stories.title: The Warlord's Last Laugh
stories.text:
Kael Stormbreaker ruled the mountain fastness for forty winters, his word written in scars and subjugation. When the young challenger Thorne ascended the high tower to face him, no one expected the old warlord to smile.

"You want my seat?" Kael's laugh was a glacier cracking. "Take it."

Thorne's blade fell true. Kael's blood steamed on the stone. But as the warlord crumpled, his grin widened—and Thorne felt the weight descend like a mountain pressing his shoulders.

Blood-oath magic. The fastness itself was bound to Kael's line through blood-oath sworn generations back. Every stone, every corridor, every torch-flame obeyed the holder of his bloodright. Thorne had won a throne built on chains.

Within days, the warriors sensed it: their new lord could not move freely. The walls resisted him. The forge-hearths guttered when he passed. By the week's end, Thorne understood Kael's final gift—not a kingdom, but a prison of stone and duty.

He laughed, bitter and true, and descended into the deep halls to learn what living architecture demanded of those it despised. The old warlord's last joke would outlive them both.

stories.title: The Echo-Broker's Gambit
stories.text: Vex moves through the shadow-markets of Korinth with a ledger bound in starlight-leather, negotiating the price of fractured memories. A refugee woman seeks the return of her sister's face—erased by the Luminous Ascension three days past. Vex can procure it, but the cost is steep: not gold, but years. The woman agrees. As Vex conducts the exchange in a cellar where the air tastes of copper and old light, she realizes the memory is counterfeit—a Void-Echo's forgery, designed to trap the buyer in perpetual hunger for what was lost. Vex tears the contract, but discovers her own curse deepening: her shadow is fragmenting, becoming multiple, as though the Shadow-Wraith is using her as a conduit to multiply itself across the night-markets. She flees into the streets, where refugees huddle in rationed camps, speaking in half-remembered languages. The Empire's authority crumbles visibly here—no guards, no ledgers, only the marketplace's cruel law.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Pilgrimage
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows stands at the edge of the Wailing Fracture, the wound in reality torn open by the harmonic crescendo of the Sunken Cathedrals. The transformed—merchants who were remade by the Stellar Rite's aftermath—move through the fracture in a state of perpetual arrival, unable to fully cross into whatever lies beyond. Kess feels the calling in her bones, a music that is not song but something deeper: the pre-human dead, singing themselves back into existence. She knows she is being used. The Shadow-Wraith whispers that if she guides the ancients' song to completion, she will be forgiven for her complicity—her identity will be restored. But Kess suspects the truth: the ritual will consume her selfhood entirely, making her into a living conduit for forces that predate humanity. She takes a step toward the fracture anyway, because her warband is singing on the other side, and she cannot leave them to sing alone.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Descent
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer has mapped the alignment of the pre-human ruins to the new constellation born from Nethis's sacrifice, and the knowledge has shattered his mind into fragments. He wanders the Bleached Wastes, speaking in the old tongue that no archivist recognizes, marking maps that show doorways where solid earth should be. A young scholar pursuing the Codex Primordial finds him delirious at a ruin-mouth, drawing circles that intersect at angles that hurt to look upon. Mardoc warns her: the ruins are not awakening—they are being *sung* awake, and the song is a summoning. The pre-human civilization that once dwelt here is not returning; something wearing their memory is clawing its way back through the gaps in reality. Before the scholar can question him further, Mardoc walks into one of his own doorways and disappears. The scholar is left holding a map that shows her destination—the Obsidian Tower where the ley-lines converge—but also shows her that arriving there will cost her more than she can afford to pay.

stories.title: The Auditor's Last Ledger
stories.text: Sevin the Auditor sits in the hidden scriptorium with forger Caldris, tasked with a new mandate: rewrite the Empire's records to match the official narrative of the Luminous Ascension. The ledgers are her tools, but as she inscribes each falsification, she feels something shifting in the act itself. Reality bends subtly around each deliberately recorded lie. A town of three hundred becomes zero; their existence is mathematically erased. Sevin realizes that bureaucratic magic has become something monstrous—that control over the written record is now control over truth itself. The Archivist-Keeper demands she continue, that she become a living archive of necessary fictions. Sevin burns the false ledger and walks into the refugee camps at Pale Crossing, where people are beginning to keep their own records outside Empire oversight, inscribing memory in defiance of institutional rewrite. Her betrayal is absolute.

stories.title: The Corsair's Confession
stories.text: Captain Veron Stormcaller, cursed by the sea and indebted to the Tear of the Drowned God, is conscripted by a faction seeking to locate fragments of the pre-Ascension world. They believe the Tear holds a map—that it is not a relic but a fragment of Nethis itself, capable of guiding the reassembly of reality. Veron navigates treacherous waters where the currents remember old routes and deep-dwellers rise from the depths to claim what was lost in the rewrite. She discovers that the Tear is *singing*, broadcasting a frequency that matches the harmonic resonance of the Sunken Cathedrals and the pre-human ruins. She realizes the truth: the Tear is not a map to the old world. It *is* the old world, compressed into an impossible artifact, and every faction that seeks it is attempting to unseal a door that should remain locked. Veron makes a choice that her crew calls mutiny: she throws the Tear overboard and watches it sink into waters that taste of starlight and salt. The curse tightens around her heart. She will die for this refusal. But perhaps that is the price of preventing something worse.

stories.title: The Wanderer's Question
stories.text: A figure claiming to be the world learning to question itself approaches multiple factions simultaneously—the Lorekeeper, the Auditors, Vex the Echo-Broker, and Ashara the Archer. Each conversation is identical in structure but different in content, tailored to the listener's deepest fears and hopes. The Wanderer asks: "If the Luminous Ascension can be reversed, should it be? If the old stars can be restored, would that restore the people who were erased, or would it merely resurrect the ghosts of what they were?" To Ashara, the Wanderer reveals knowledge of her warband—names, faces, songs—that should have been erased. This suggests the Shadow-Wraith is either incomplete or merciful, or that the Wanderer itself is a fragment of Nethis attempting to communicate something the cosmic entity could not express before its sacrifice. By the end of the issue, it becomes clear that the Wanderer is orchestrating something—guiding factions toward convergence at the Obsidian Tower where ley-lines burn and the imprisoned thing beneath begins to sing.

stories.title: The Merchant's Transformation
stories.text: A spice trader from the Scorched Reach has become something between human and transformed after the Stellar Rite rewrite. His body now exists partially in the daylight world and partially in the liminal space of the Wailing Fracture. He experiences both simultaneously: morning light and perpetual twilight of arrival at the Sunken Cathedrals. His true curse is deeper—he retains perfect memory of the world before the rewrite, but cannot prove it existed. He knows faces of the erased, streets that no longer appear on maps, conversations in languages that died. A young scribe finds him in a market that exists in two places at once. He offers her a choice: learn the location of a faction seeking to complete the pre-human ritual, or accept a gift—knowledge of her own erased childhood. She chooses knowledge. The trader smiles, and in the smile she sees something vast and celestial looking through his eyes, as though Nethis itself is present in his transformed flesh.

stories.title: The Shade-Echo's Hunger
stories.text: A Void-Echo—a fragment of Nethis's sacrificed consciousness—has taken residence in the body of a scholar who was becoming a vessel for memory. But unlike previous Echoes, this one retains enough of the scholar's personality to argue with itself. Half the time it remembers being human; the other half it hungers to consume memories and complete its own fragmentation into wholeness. It wanders the borderlands between the Burned Expanse and the Hearthlands, stealing moments from refugees and destroying its own thefts in cycles of self-sabotage. Ashara the Archer is tracking it—she has made a tentative pact with this Echo, believing it can be reasoned with, that it might help her recover her warband's names before they are fully erased. But the Echo is disintegrating. Soon there will be no scholar left to negotiate with, only hunger. Ashara must choose: let the memory be devoured, allowing the Echo a moment of clarity and peace, or fight to preserve a moment that no longer matters in a world that has been rewritten.

stories.title: The Young Sorcerer's Tower
stories.text: The young sorcerer who inherited forbidden knowledge now stands at the Obsidian Tower where ley-lines converge in impossible geometries. The Tower was sealed centuries ago to contain something—a being, a working, a wound in the world—and the young sorcerer was tasked with guarding it. But the ley-line convergence triggered by the Stellar Rite is breaking the seal from the inside. Something is singing to be let out. The young sorcerer has discovered, among the Tower's archives, fragments of the Codex Primordial describing a Stellar Rite reversal—a working that could theoretically undo Zareth's sacrifice and restore the old world. But the reversal requires a price equal in magnitude: another cosmic entity must be willing to sacrifice its consciousness, and the only entity available is the imprisoned thing beneath the Tower. If the sorcerer releases it, it might agree to the reversal. Or it might simply consume the world. The sorcerer stands at the sealing-stone, hands trembling, as the Tower shakes with the imprisoned thing's song.
